sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 43, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 42 of about 55 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The UP transition shows:
- Before: node 13=white (100px), node 14=charcoal (63px)
- After: node 13=white (100px), node 14=charcoal (62px)
So UP only changed the bottom bar (node 14) from 63px to 62px. The frame (node 11) is still gray. The purple appearance in the image is because node 12 (purple) is visible through the frame area - the frame is just a border, not a filled rectangle.
Wait, but the current state shows node 13=purple (50px), node 14=white (50px). This is different from the UP transition which showed node 13=white (100px). This means there were more actions after the UP transition that I'm looking at.
Let me re-examine. The transitions list has multiple entries. The UP transition I found was the FIRST UP action, not the most recent one. Let me look at the most recent transitions.
Actually, looking at the current state:
- Node 12: purple (84px)
- Node 13: purple (50px) 
- Node 14: white (50px)
This is the state after LEFT, UP, UP. The LEFT rotated node 12 back to rectangle. The two UP actions only changed the bottom bar.
But wait - the current state has 3 blocks (node 12, 13, 14), not 2. How did we get to 3 blocks? Let me trace back:
- Before the sequence: node 12=white (84px), node 13=white (100px) [2 blocks]
- MOUSE purple: node 12=purple, node 13=white (100px)
- SPACE: split → node 12=purple (84px), node 13=purple (50px), node 14=white (50px) [3 blocks]
- LEFT: rotated node 12
- UP, UP: only changed bottom bar
So the current state is: purple (84px), purple (50px), white (50px). The bottom bar is changing with each action.
Now, the panel shows white on top, purple on bottom. The main area has purple on top, purple-white below. 
I'm running out of actions (42 of ~55). Let me think about what the goal might be.
Looking at the image again:
- Panel: white block on top, purple block on bottom
- Main area: purple block in frame, purple block below, white block below that
Maybe the goal is to have the main area's blocks match the panel's arrangement. The panel has white-purple (top to bottom). The main area needs to have white-purple too.
Currently the main area has:
- Node 12: purple (in frame)
- Node 13: purple (below frame)
- Node 14: white (below node 13)
If I ignore node 12 (which is in the frame and might be a "color selector"), the bottom area has purple-white. I need white-purple.
But I can't directly swap the order of node 13 and node 14. SPACE merges or splits, but doesn't swap.
Wait, maybe the goal is different. Maybe the goal is to have the bottom area be a single block of a specific color. Or maybe the goal involves the frame.
Let me try a different approach. Let me see if there's a pattern in the bottom bar changes that indicates progress. The bottom bar seems to decrease with each action.
Actually, I just realized something. Let me look at the panel more carefully. The panel has TWO blocks: white and purple. The main area has blocks that can be manipulated. Maybe the goal is to make the main area have the same TWO blocks as the panel, in the same order.
The panel shows: white (top), purple (bottom).
To achieve this in the main area:
- Node 13 should be white (50px)
- Node 14 should be purple (50px)
Current state: node 13=purple (50px), node 14=white (50px) - reversed!
How to swap them? Let me think...
1. Merge them: SPACE with node 12=purple, node 13=purple → same color, no change
2. Change node 12 to white: now node 12=white, node 13=purple → different colors, SPACE merges
3. After merge: node 13=white (100px) [takes node 12's color]
4. Now node 12=white, node 13=white → same color, SPACE doesn't work
5. Change node 12 to purple: now node 12=purple, node 13=white → different colors, SPACE splits
6. After split: node 13=purple (50px), node 14=white (50px) - back to original
This doesn't help. I can't swap the order.
Hmm, what if I need to change the bottom bar? Or what if the goal involves the frame?
Let me try clicking on the panel blocks to see what happens. Maybe clicking the panel changes something.
Actually wait - let me re-examine. Maybe the goal is to have node 12 (in the frame) match the panel's top color (white), and the bottom area (nodes 13+14) match the panel's bottom color (purple).
So the goal state would be:
- Node 12: white
- Node 13: purple (single block, 100px)
Current state:
- Node 12: purple
- Node 13: purple (50px)
- Node 14: white (50px)
To get there:
1. Merge node 13+14: need node 12 ≠ node 13. Currently both purple. Change node 12 to white.
2. SPACE: merges → node 13=white (100px) [takes node 12's color]
3. Now node 12=white, node 13=white. Need node 13=purple.
4. Change node 12 to purple.
5. SPACE: splits → node 13=purple (50px), node 14=white (50px)
6. Now need to merge again... but node 12=purple, node 13=purple → same, no change.
I'm going in circles. Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe I need to use the panel differently.
Let me try clicking on the panel's purple block to see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current state: node 12=purple (84px), node 13=purple (50px), node 14=white (50px)
# The bottom bar (node 14/15) changes with each action
# UP/DOWN only affect the bottom bar
# LEFT/RIGHT rotate node 12
# SPACE splits/merges based on node 12 vs node 13 colors
# MOUSE changes node 12's color
#
# Let me check the bottom bar across all transitions to find a pattern
print("Bottom bar across transitions:")
for i, t in enumerate(transitions[-10:]):
    before = t.before_frame.segmentation
    after = t.after_frame.segmentation
    # Find bottom bar node (largest row in boundary)
    for n in after['nodes']:
        if n['boundary'] and max([p[0] for p in n['boundary']]) >= 60:
            print(f"  After {t.action}: id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']}")
            break

# Let me also check if there's a node 15 now
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAll nodes with large row values:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['boundary']:
        max_row = max([p[0] for p in n['boundary']])
        if max_row >= 60:
            print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Let me try clicking on the panel's purple block (node 9)
# Node 9 boundary: [[8, 3], [8, 12], [12, 12], [12, 3]]
# Center is around row 10, col 7
predict(expect_change=True, note='Click panel purple block')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 10, 'col': 7}])

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter clicking panel purple:")
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [9, 12, 13, 14]:
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current state: node 12=purple (84px), node 13=purple (50px), node 14=white (50px)
